mnemonics.title: LLK - Lazy Lion Kicks
mnemonics.content: LL(k) = Left-to-right scanning, Leftmost derivation, k lookahead tokens. Remember: 'Lazy Lion Kicks' - the lion (parser) lazily looks ahead k times before making decisions.

mnemonics.title: LR - Lions Roar Back
mnemonics.content: LR Parser = Left-to-right scanning, Rightmost derivation in reverse. Remember: 'Lions Roar' - the bottom-up parser roars back from the end to build the tree.

mnemonics.title: FIRST/FOLLOW - Fish Fry Or Food
mnemonics.content: FIRST = Terminals that can start a derivation. FOLLOW = Terminals that can follow a non-terminal in any valid sentential form. Remember: 'Fish Fry Or Food' - what comes FIRST in the pan, you eat FOLLOWing it.

mnemonics.title: Recursive Descent - Dive Deep Doer
mnemonics.content: Recursive Descent Parser: Uses recursive functions for each non-terminal. Remember: 'Dive Deep' - you recursively dive deeper into each production rule.

mnemonics.title: Shift-Reduce - Shifting Shopping Reduces Bag
mnemonics.content: Shift-Reduce Parser: Shifts input tokens onto stack, then reduces using grammar rules. Remember: 'Shifting Shopping Reduces Bag' - you shift (add) items while shopping, then reduce (checkout) your bag.

mnemonics.title: Predictive Parser - Plan Before Play
mnemonics.content: Predictive Parsing: No backtracking, uses lookahead to predict correct production. Remember: 'Plan Before Play' - like chess, you plan your move (production) before playing it.

acronyms.usage: Used for top-down parsing. LL(1) is most common - uses 1 token lookahead. Suitable for deterministic context-free grammars.

acronyms.expansion: Left-to-right, Rightmost derivation in reverse, k lookahead
acronyms.usage: Used for bottom-up parsing. More powerful than LL. LR(1) uses 1 token lookahead. Can handle more grammars.
A compact version of LR(1). Merges states with same lookahead. Used in Yacc/Bison. Good balance between power and table size.
Simplest LR parser. Uses Follow sets to resolve conflicts. Less powerful than LR(1) but simpler to construct.
Tree representation of parse tree without unnecessary nodes. Used in compilers for semantic analysis and code generation.
Formal grammar where left side has single non-terminal. Basis for most programming language syntax.
Metasyntax notation for CFGs. Used to define programming language syntax (e.g., in language manuals).

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an upside-down family tree. The root (Start Symbol) is at the top, branches grow downward. Terminals are leaves at the bottom. Like a tree diagram showing how a sentence is built from grammar rules.
Think of a treasure hunt. You start at the treasure (start symbol) and dig deeper into branches until you reach the gold (terminals). You choose which branch to take based on lookahead.
Think of folding a blanket. You start with flat corners (terminals) at bottom and keep folding upward (reducing) until you reach the compact bundle (start symbol) at top.
Imagine a plate stack in a restaurant. Servers 'shift' new plates onto top, then 'reduce' by serving (removing) a complete set when ready. Stack grows/shrinks from top.
Think of Russian nesting dolls. Each function opens to reveal smaller dolls (non-terminals) inside until you reach the solid center (terminals).

First = Who comes FIRST at a party (terminals that start). Follow = Who stands ACROSS (FOLLOW) from you at the party (terminals that can appear after). Two circles showing who enters first vs who stands nearby.

analogies.analogy: Like following a recipe to cook. Each ingredient (non-terminal) leads to sub-recipes (recursive calls), which lead to actual ingredients (terminals). You follow step-by-step instructions.
Like choosing a door in a maze with only 1 peek ahead. You look at the next symbol and immediately know which door to take. No guessing, no backtracking.
Like assembling IKEA furniture in reverse. You start with finished pieces (terminals), then combine them step-by-step until you have the complete product (start symbol).
Like the sentence 'I saw a man with a telescope.' Did you use a telescope to see the man, or did the man have a telescope? Multiple valid parse trees = ambiguous.
At a buffet, you can't decide whether to take more food (shift) or stop eating (reduce). The parser faces the same dilemma when grammar rules overlap.
You have two different recipe books and can't decide which one to follow for the same dish. Parser has multiple valid reductions - confusing!
Like trying to climb an infinite ladder that curves back on itself. A → A α keeps going left forever. Top-down parsers get stuck in infinite loop. Fix by removing left recursion.
Parse tree is a full family photo with everyone (including distant cousins). AST is just your immediate family - removes unnecessary people but keeps important relationships.
